title: Decision Bias
puzzle: Why do smart people still make predictably flawed decisions?
insight: Human judgment relies on shortcuts that save time but can distort reasoning. These systematic errors are known as cognitive biases.
key_ideas: Biases often come from fast mental shortcuts.
They can affect memory, risk, and confidence.
Awareness helps, but does not erase them automatically.
mind_blower: The brain is not mainly built to be perfectly rational. It is built to decide quickly under pressure.
next_curiosity: If bias is built into judgment, how can people design better decisions?
narration_text: Why do smart people still make predictably flawed decisions? Human judgment relies on shortcuts that save time but can distort reasoning. These systematic errors are known as cognitive biases. Biases often come from fast mental shortcuts. They can affect memory, risk, and confidence. Awareness helps, but does not erase them automatically. The brain is not mainly built to be perfectly rational. It is built to decide quickly under pressure. If bias is built into judgment, how can people design better decisions?
